comment_text.sn10.3:1.2: “Shaggy” is khara and “Spiky” is sūciloma, literally “needle-hair”. Some yakkhas are magnificent kings or warriors, but this pair are more like grotesque demons (rakkhasas), a stereotype that bedeviled yakṣas of later times. Khara is found for a number of demons in Sanskrit. We don’t find suciloma, but compare the demons Khara and his twin sister, the ugly and mutilated Śūrpaṇakhā (“wicker-nails”).
comment_text.sn10.3:2.8: Considering the Ganges is 100 km away, this is even more impressive.
comment_text.sn10.3:3.1: Despite their savage demeanor, they pivot right away to deep Dhamma questions. This kind of rapid pivot is characteristic of such conversion narratives.
comment_text.sn10.3:5.2: The banyan is “trunk-born” (khandhaja) in two ways. As an epiphyte, it is sometimes called the “strangler fig”, since it grows around the trunk of a previous tree, strangling it (much like the māluva below). Then the banyan sends out aerial roots from the branches; when they reach the ground they establish themselves as supportive trunks known as “prop roots”, so that even if original trunk dies, the tree survives. Thus the tree itself is “trunk-born” and its multiple prop roots are also “trunk-born”. Grammar and commentary indicate the second sense is meant here. The metaphor is even more apt, since the word nigrodha (“banyan”) means “down-grower”, as the tree grows not to the sky but back into the earth.
comment_text.sn10.3:5.4: The “camel’s foot creeper” (māluva) takes root and grows over the tree, ultimately engulfing and destroying it (MN 45:4.1).

root_text.sn10.3:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā gayāyaṁ viharati ṭaṅkitamañce sūcilomassa yakkhassa bhavane.
root_text.sn10.3:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena kharo ca yakkho sūcilomo ca yakkho bhagavato avidūre atikkamanti.
root_text.sn10.3:1.3: Atha kho kharo yakkho sūcilomaṁ yakkhaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.3:1.6: Yāva jānāmi yadi vā so samaṇo yadi vā pana so samaṇako”ti.
root_text.sn10.3:2.1: Atha kho sūcilomo yakkho yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavato kāyaṁ upanāmesi.
root_text.sn10.3:2.3: Atha kho sūcilomo yakkho bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.3:2.8: Sace me na byākarissasi, cittaṁ vā te khipissāmi, hadayaṁ vā te phālessāmi, pādesu vā gahetvā pāragaṅgāya khipissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn10.3:2.9: “Na khvāhaṁ taṁ, āvuso, passāmi sadevake loke samārake sabrahmake sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiyā pajāya sadevamanussāya, yo me cittaṁ vā khipeyya hadayaṁ vā phāleyya pādesu vā gahetvā pāragaṅgāya khipeyya;
root_text.sn10.3:2.10: api ca tvaṁ, āvuso, puccha yadā kaṅkhasī”ti. (…)

translation_text.sn10.3:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Gayā on the cut-stone ledge in the haunt of Spiky the native spirit.
translation_text.sn10.3:1.2: Now at that time the native spirits Shaggy and Spiky were passing by not far from the Buddha.
translation_text.sn10.3:1.6: I’ll soon find out whether he’s an ascetic or a faker.”
translation_text.sn10.3:2.1: Then Spiky went up to the Buddha and leaned up against his body,
translation_text.sn10.3:2.3: Then Spiky said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn10.3:2.7: “I will ask you a question, ascetic.
translation_text.sn10.3:2.8: If you don’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane, or explode your heart, or grab you by the feet and throw you to the far shore of the Ganges!”
translation_text.sn10.3:2.9: “I don’t see anyone in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans who could do that to me.
translation_text.sn10.3:2.10: But anyway, ask what you wish.”
translation_text.sn10.3:3.1: “Where do greed and hate come from?
translation_text.sn10.3:3.2: From where spring discontent, desire, and terror?
translation_text.sn10.3:3.3: Where do the mind’s thoughts originate,
translation_text.sn10.3:3.4: like a crow let loose by boys.”
translation_text.sn10.3:4.2: from here spring discontent, desire, and terror;
translation_text.sn10.3:4.3: here’s where the mind’s thoughts originate,
translation_text.sn10.3:4.4: like a crow let loose by boys.
translation_text.sn10.3:5.1: Born of affection, originating in oneself,
translation_text.sn10.3:5.2: like prop roots sprouting from a banyan;
translation_text.sn10.3:5.3: the many kinds of attachment to sensual pleasures
translation_text.sn10.3:5.4: are like camel’s foot creeper <j>creeping through the woods.
translation_text.sn10.3:6.1: Those who understand where they come from
translation_text.sn10.3:6.2: get rid of them—listen up, spirit!
translation_text.sn10.3:6.3: They cross this flood so hard to cross,
translation_text.sn10.3:6.4: not crossed before, so as to not be reborn.”

variant_text.sn10.3:2.10: (…) → (atha kho sūcilomo yakkho bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi.) (bj, pts2ed)

reference_text.sn10.3:1.1: cck15.267, csp1ed12.209, csp2ed12.209, ms12S1_1459, msdiv237, ndp12.208, pts-vp-pli1ed1.207
